tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with README.md to learn what R+ is: an Electron desktop workbench for hospital guardia, built local-first with Spanish UI and structured .docx export from pasted clinical text. Pair it with docs/core/00-system-index.md — the documentation hub that maps every feature domain, logic module, and database area you will touch during a shift.

tour.title: Product North Star
tour.description: Before reading code, read docs/core/01-vision-north-star.md to understand the trade-offs that shaped R+: lower time-to-documentation, LiveSync trust on the ward LAN, and human-in-the-loop clinical safety. Knowing the anti-goals (no cloud PHI, no EMR replacement) explains why architecture choices like SQLCipher, LAN-only sync, and paste-to-structure parsers exist.

tour.title: Electron Main Process
tour.description: main.js is the desktop entry point scored highest by graph topology. It creates the BrowserWindow, wires IPC channels to the clinical database, spawns the LAN HTTP server on port 3738, and manages auto-updates. Every renderer action that touches encrypted storage or native modules ultimately routes through handlers registered here.
tour.languageLesson: Electron splits work across a Node main process (filesystem, native modules, window lifecycle) and sandboxed renderer processes (UI). preload.js bridges them safely via contextBridge.

tour.title: LAN HTTP Server
tour.description: Building on main.js, server.js runs the ward-facing Express server: document export routes, interno mobile board APIs, and the WebSocket hub that peers use for LiveSync. When multiple Macs share a guardia turn, this process is the coordination point — clients push patient bundles here and subscribe to real-time updates.

tour.title: Renderer Boot & IPC
tour.description: The UI layer starts in public/js/app.js, which bootstraps the bundled renderer and delegates feature registration to app-runtimes.mjs. preload.js exposes window.electronAPI — the typed IPC surface the renderer uses to unlock SQLCipher, export documents, and query LAN status without direct Node access. Together these three files define how a resident's clicks become main-process actions.

tour.title: SQLCipher Clinical Database
tour.description: Patient data lives in an encrypted SQLite database managed by lib/db/db-manager.mjs with schema definitions and migrations in lib/db/schema.mjs. ipc-handlers.mjs registers the IPC channels main.js exposes — teams, guardia rotation, clinical privileges, and audit logging all persist through this layer. Understanding schema.mjs first tells you which tables back the census, labs sidecars, and handoff state you see in the UI.
tour.languageLesson: SQLCipher wraps SQLite with transparent page-level encryption. R+ derives keys via Argon2 — the same native binary must be used in tests (Electron's embedded Node) and at runtime.

tour.title: Shared Application State
tour.description: app-state.mjs ranks highest in fan-in across the renderer — 52 modules depend on it for the active patient, census hydration, and turn-wide context. clinical-access-runtime.mjs layers session identity and privilege checks on top, deciding what an R1 can edit versus what requires a senior or admin scope. These two modules are the glue between DB-backed storage and every feature panel you open during guardia.

tour.title: LAN Sync Host Layer
tour.description: LiveSync durability lives in lan-squad/: host-store.js holds the room census and clinical ops bundles, host-router.js serves REST endpoints for push/pull and patient deletes, and ws-hub.js broadcasts deltas to connected peers. These modules sit directly under server.js in the dependency graph and implement the host-side merge, conflict resolution, and persistence barriers that keep every Mac on the ward consistent through a 24-hour turn.

tour.title: LAN Sync Client
tour.description: On the renderer side, features/lan/orchestrator.mjs is the LiveSync façade — it wires push, room join, delta apply, and patient-delete flows to the host. The thin barrel lan-sync.mjs re-exports the public API that app-runtimes registers. When you edit a patient and see changes appear on a colleague's screen, orchestrator.mjs is coordinating the round-trip through the host layer from Step 8.

tour.title: Patient Census & Teams
tour.description: features/patients.mjs drives the censo — admit, bed assignment, sidebar cards, and LAN-scoped patient lists that dominate daily guardia workflow. clinical-teams/index.mjs connects roster, guardia rotation, and team-scoped visibility so each resident sees only their team's patients on the board and iPad mirror. These are the first feature modules you interact with when starting a shift.

tour.title: Labs & Estado Actual
tour.description: labs.js is R+'s core paste-to-structure engine — it parses SOME and laboratory report text into typed sections (BH, gases, cultivo, coag) with 42 dependents across the codebase. estado-actual-data.mjs and estado-actual-parser.mjs handle vitals, I/O balance, and monitoring registro — the data you chart hourly on guardia. Together they power the magic moment: raw hospital text becomes structured, syncable clinical data.

tour.title: Document Export & Build
tour.description: When documentation is ready, lib/doc-generators/note.js (with indicaciones and listado siblings) produces native .docx files, served over HTTP by lib/doc-export-http.js from the LAN server in Step 4. package.json ties the stack together — Electron 41, esbuild bundling via npm run build:ui, and SQLCipher native rebuilds. This capstone shows how parsed labs and estado actual data exit the app as ward-ready notes.
tour.languageLesson: R+ uses esbuild to bundle public/js/**/*.mjs into app.bundle.mjs. After any renderer edit you must run npm run build:ui — never hand-edit generated chunks.
